However, I can suggest a similar plant, "Indigofera indica" (Indian indigo or true indigo), which is a type of flowering plant in the pea family. Here's a paragraph about it:

Indigofera indica, also known as Indian indigo or true indigo, is a perennial flowering plant native to tropical Asia. The plant has been used for centuries in traditional medicine, with health benefits including antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which can help to soothe skin irritations and boost the immune system. The bioactive constituents of Indigofera indica include indigotin, a pigment with potential anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory effects, as well as other flavonoids and phenolic acids. Indigofera indica is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and topical creams, which are valued for their ability to promote wound healing, reduce pain and inflammation, and protect against oxidative stress.
benefits: Indurgia indica is known for its potential health benefits, which include reducing inflammation and improving cardiovascular health due to its high antioxidant and flavonoid content. The plant is also believed to have anti-diabetic properties, helping to regulate blood sugar levels. Additionally, it may help to protect against oxidative stress and cell damage caused by free radicals. Indurgia indica has been traditionally used in various cultures to treat various health conditions, including fever and respiratory issues. Further research is needed to fully understand its potential health benefits and confirm its efficacy in treating various diseases.
